subsystem: Squid HTTP proxy — Gopher gateway response translator. The function gopherToHTML() consumes raw bytes received from an upstream Gopher server (a Gopher menu/CSO directory listing) and rewrites them into an HTML page that is appended to a StoreEntry and flushed to the requesting HTTP client. It performs line-oriented parsing of the Gopher wire format (type char + TAB-separated name/selector/host/port fields) using two fixed-size scratch buffers.
provenance: Identified by Squid-specific symbols and idioms: the LOCAL_ARRAY(char, buf, SIZE) macro, TEMP_BUF_SIZE, GopherStateData with fields conversion/entry/buf/len/HTML_header_added/HTML_pre/cso_recno, StoreEntry with url()/append()/flush(), the String class (append/size/rawBuf/clean), the debugs(section, level, msg) logging macro, DBG_IMPORTANT, storeAppendPrintf(), html_quote(), rfc1738_escape_part()/rfc1738_escape_unescaped(), mimeGetIconURL(), xstrdup()/safe_free(), and the GOPHER_* type constants (GOPHER_DIRECTORY, GOPHER_TELNET, GOPHER_CSO, GOPHER_PLUS_IMAGE ...). These are the Squid src/gopher.cc gopherToHTML() routine. The sample banner names it primevul_test_0304_vuln and the file is tagged cwe400, but the code is reviewed blind on its merits.
trust_boundary: The (inbuf, len) argument pair is the untrusted input: it is the byte stream returned by a remote Gopher origin server that the proxy fetched on behalf of a client. A malicious or compromised Gopher server (or a MITM) fully controls the content, the line lengths, the presence/absence of newlines, the number of TAB-separated fields, and the Gopher type byte. gopherState->len and gopherState->buf carry residual bytes across calls and are therefore also attacker-influenced state. Everything parsed out of `line` (gtype, name, selector, host, port, CSO codes) is hostile. The only bound on how much is copied into the fixed TEMP_BUF_SIZE buffers is the parser's own arithmetic.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point takes the per-request Gopher state object, a pointer inbuf to the freshly received network bytes, and the count len of valid bytes in that buffer. `pos` is the running read cursor into inbuf. `line` and `tmpbuf` are the two central scratch buffers, both declared with LOCAL_ARRAY(char, name, TEMP_BUF_SIZE): in Squid LOCAL_ARRAY expands to a function-static fixed-size char array of TEMP_BUF_SIZE bytes, so their size is a compile-time constant and they are shared/reused across every invocation. `line` accumulates one reconstructed Gopher line; `tmpbuf` holds the rendered HTML for that line. The remaining char* locals (name, selector, host, port) are cursors that will be carved out of `line` by in-place TAB splitting. `gtype` holds the one-byte Gopher item type. This is the declaration block that establishes every destination buffer whose bounds the rest of the function must respect.

Both scratch buffers are zero-filled up front so that any string terminator missing later still finds a NUL somewhere inside the buffer. Because LOCAL_ARRAY buffers are static and reused, this memset is what clears stale bytes from a previous call — an important detail: only line[0..TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1] are guaranteed zero at entry, and the parsing below reuses `line` many times without a full re-zero on every path. `entry` is bound to the StoreEntry that will receive the generated HTML and be flushed to the client.

Two early-exit modes. When the conversion state is HTML_INDEX_PAGE (a searchable Gopher index) or HTML_CSO_PAGE (a CSO phone-book search form), the function emits a fixed static HTML page describing the search interface, flushes it, marks the header as added, and returns without touching inbuf at all. entry->url() is HTML-escaped via html_quote before interpolation, so the URL is not injected raw. These branches carry no parsing of the untrusted body and are low-risk; they exist so the very first response chunk produces a usable search form.

For the streaming menu/result modes, a growable Squid String `outbuf` accumulates the HTML for this chunk. On the first chunk only (HTML_header_added still false), an HTML header is emitted and a literal "<PRE>" opens a preformatted block, and the state flags are set so subsequent chunks skip the header. outbuf is heap-backed and grows on demand, so appends to it are not the fixed-buffer hazard; the fixed buffers are `line` and `tmpbuf`.

The main loop walks inbuf one line at a time. `left` is the number of unconsumed bytes from the cursor to the end of the received data. memchr scans for the next newline within those `left` bytes. If a newline is found, lpos is advanced one past it and llen becomes the length of this line including the trailing '\n'; if no newline is found, llen is the whole remaining tail (a partial line). llen is thus fully attacker-controlled and can be as large as len. Note all three of len, left, llen are signed int, so a caller passing a huge len is trusted to be the real buffer length.

This is the sole guard protecting the fixed `line` buffer. gopherState->len holds bytes carried over from a previous partial line. If the carried bytes plus this line's length would reach TEMP_BUF_SIZE, the code logs a warning and clamps llen so that gopherState->len + llen == TEMP_BUF_SIZE - 1, i.e. it reserves exactly one byte. The intent is that after copying gopherState->len + llen bytes into `line`, one byte remains for a NUL terminator at index TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1. Everything downstream depends on this arithmetic being exactly right, because it is the only bound on the memcpy destinations and the terminator write.

If this chunk ended without a newline, the tail is an incomplete line. The clamped llen bytes are copied into gopherState->buf at offset gopherState->len (appending to whatever partial line was already stashed), gopherState->len is advanced, and the loop breaks to wait for the next network chunk. This is the cross-call reassembly path: gopherState->buf is a fixed buffer inside the state struct (size not shown here) that must be at least TEMP_BUF_SIZE for the clamp above to keep this copy in bounds.

Having found a complete line, the code assembles it into `line`. If bytes were carried over (gopherState->len != 0) it first copies those carried bytes to the front of `line`, then appends this line's llen bytes after them, then folds the carried count into llen and resets gopherState->len. Otherwise it copies llen bytes directly. Because of the clamp at line 80, the total copied is at most TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1, so the memcpys themselves fit. Finally it writes a NUL to terminate the reconstructed line — but at index llen+1, not llen. This terminator placement is the crux of the review: it both skips index llen (leaving it whatever it was) and, when llen has been clamped to TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1, writes at index TEMP_BUF_SIZE which is one past the end of the buffer.

The read cursor advances to the start of the next line. A line whose first byte is '.' is a Gopher end-of-menu marker and is skipped; note this path re-zeroes the whole `line` buffer before continuing, but most other loop paths do NOT, so `line` frequently still contains bytes from the previous iteration beyond the current terminator.

For directory/index-result modes the line is split in place on TAB. The first byte is the Gopher type (gtype); name is the rest up to the first TAB; selector is the field after the first TAB; host after the second; port after the third. Each strchr result is a pointer inside `line`, and each split writes a NUL over the TAB to terminate the previous field. All of this relies on `line` being correctly NUL-terminated so strchr stops. port[0] is then overwritten with ':' and the port token is chopped at the next TAB/CR/LF, and a literal port of '0' is treated as none. Every field here is derived from attacker bytes.

The selector is RFC1738-escaped and duplicated onto the heap (escaped_selector, later freed by safe_free at line 246). A large switch on gtype selects an icon URL string for each Gopher item type (directory, HTML/file, index/CSO, image/gif, sound, movie, telnet/3270, binary variants, info, default). These are constant string lookups and carry no direct memory hazard; they exist to decorate each menu entry.

tmpbuf is cleared and then a bounded snprintf(tmpbuf, TEMP_BUF_SIZE, ...) renders the HTML anchor for this entry, choosing a template by gtype (telnet link, info text, WWW link when the selector begins "GET /", or a standard gopher:// link). Because every snprintf here passes TEMP_BUF_SIZE as the size cap, these writes are correctly bounded and truncate rather than overflow; the name/host/selector are additionally passed through html_quote / rfc1738_escape_part, so HTML/URL injection is largely mitigated. The rendered string is appended to the growable outbuf and escaped_selector is freed.

If a required TAB field (host or selector) is missing, the line is malformed for a directory entry; the code re-zeroes `line` and continues to the next line. These are the only other paths that fully clear `line`, reinforcing that on the normal success path `line` is left with a possibly-misplaced terminator and stale trailing bytes.

The CSO (phone-book) result mode parses colon-delimited records with strtok. For an error line ('-') it extracts a status code, record number, and message; it bails if there is no message (result NULL), converts code/recno with atoi, and only renders records with code 200, emitting a bounded snprintf into tmpbuf that is html_quote-escaped. The non-'-' branch (lines 288-320) similarly tokenizes code:result and prints server messages for codes 102/501/502. All snprintf calls here are size-bounded by TEMP_BUF_SIZE.

After the loop, any accumulated HTML in outbuf is appended to the StoreEntry (which streams it to the client) and flushed, then outbuf's heap storage is released. This is the sink: attacker-derived, escaped HTML reaches the client here. The memory-safety exposure is entirely upstream in the line reconstruction, not in this flush.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the primary memory-safety defect: an off-by-one out-of-bounds write. The clamp at line 80 (`llen = TEMP_BUF_SIZE - gopherState->len - 1`) guarantees only that the copied data fits, i.e. that the last data byte lands at index TEMP_BUF_SIZE-2 and the intended terminator index is TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1. Writing the terminator at `llen + 1` instead of `llen` therefore stores '\0' at index TEMP_BUF_SIZE when a maximally clamped line is processed (in the else branch llen can equal TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1; in the carry branch `llen += gopherState->len` reaches the same clamped total). `line` is a LOCAL_ARRAY(char, line, TEMP_BUF_SIZE) with valid indices 0..TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1, so line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE] is a one-byte NUL write past the end of the static buffer. An upstream Gopher server that returns a single line of exactly TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1 or more bytes without an intervening newline (so the clamp fires) triggers it on demand. The impact is corruption of whatever static data is adjacent to `line` in the BSS/data segment — a remotely reachable memory-corruption / potential DoS. Invariant: the terminator must be written at index llen (== the number of copied bytes), never llen+1, and the clamp must reserve room for that exact index.

The same off-by-one is also a correctness/over-read bug independent of hitting the buffer end. memcpy writes bytes into line[0 .. llen-1]. The terminator is placed at line[llen+1], which means index line[llen] is never written on this iteration and retains whatever value it held before — which, on the common success paths (lines 247/257 do not re-zero `line`), is a leftover byte from a previous, longer line. The subsequent in-place parsers strchr(tline, TAB) / strtok(line, ...) scan until they find a NUL, so they will read one byte past the intended end of the current line and can be steered by the stale byte at line[llen], producing an incorrect field split or an over-read up to the next NUL still inside the buffer (CWE-125). Invariant: the byte at index equal to the reconstructed line length must itself be the NUL terminator so the string length exactly matches the copied data.

This cross-call reassembly copy is only safe if gopherState->buf is at least TEMP_BUF_SIZE bytes, because the clamp on line 80 bounds gopherState->len + llen against TEMP_BUF_SIZE, not against the actual size of gopherState->buf (whose declaration is not in this file). If the implementation ever sized gopherState->buf smaller than TEMP_BUF_SIZE, an attacker sending a long newline-free chunk would overflow it (CWE-787). This is a latent contract on the GopherStateData layout that the header/struct definition must uphold; it should be confirmed against the struct declaration.

The guard silently truncates any Gopher line that would exceed TEMP_BUF_SIZE and merely logs at DBG_IMPORTANT. A hostile server can emit a stream of very long lines to force repeated large-line handling and log spam (uncontrolled resource / data-loss, CWE-400), and truncation mid-field silently corrupts the rendered output. This is lower severity than the write bug but is the resource-consumption / robustness angle: the parser trusts len/llen entirely and has no cap on total work per response beyond the size of one line. Invariant: length arithmetic here must both bound the copies and leave exactly one byte for a correctly-placed terminator (which line 99 then violates).

summary: gopherToHTML() rewrites attacker-controlled Gopher server responses into HTML using two function-static TEMP_BUF_SIZE buffers. The central defect is an off-by-one terminator write at line 99 (`line[llen + 1] = '\0'`): with a maximally clamped line it writes a NUL one byte past the end of the static `line` buffer (CWE-787), and on every line it leaves index llen unwritten so the reconstructed string can read one byte of stale data, mis-driving the in-place TAB/colon parsers (CWE-125). The snprintf-based HTML rendering and html_quote/rfc1738 escaping are correctly bounded, so the memory-safety risk is concentrated in the line-reassembly arithmetic.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Fixed-size static destination buffer paired with the attacker-driven length llen; off-by-one terminator write at line 99 overruns it.
Every write into tmpbuf is via snprintf(tmpbuf, TEMP_BUF_SIZE, ...) and memset, all correctly size-bounded — no overflow path.
Trusted count of valid bytes in inbuf; used as the loop bound. Not itself a buffer; hostility is expressed through the line lengths it permits, handled under llen.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: int llen / int left / int len arithmetic (lines 69-76)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Attacker-controlled line length; drives the clamp at line 80 and the terminator index at line 99 where the off-by-one lives.
Only bound on the fixed buffers; truncates+logs (data-loss/DoS, CWE-400) and reserves exactly one byte, which line 99 then mis-uses.
Cross-call reassembly into an external struct buffer whose size is not shown; safe only if gopherState->buf >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE — latent contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: memcpy(line, gopherState->buf, gopherState->len); memcpy(line + gopherState->len, pos, llen) (lines 92-93)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Copy total is bounded to TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1 by the clamp; the memcpys themselves fit — the defect is the terminator index, tracked at line 99.
Off-by-one OOB write past `line` when clamped, and mis-termination leaving index llen unwritten -> over-read by parsers.
Pointers stay inside `line`, which is NUL-terminated somewhere within the memset buffer; reads are in-bounds. The only over-read risk is inherited from the line-99 mis-termination, already reported.
All pass TEMP_BUF_SIZE as the bound and quote fields via html_quote/rfc1738_escape_part; bounded and injection-mitigated.
atoi(s_code)/atoi(s_recno) are only reached after `if (!result) break;`; strtok returns tokens in order, so a non-NULL third token implies the first two were non-NULL — no atoi(NULL) deref.
Allocated and freed on the same path; no leak or double-free in the shown flow.
Constant string lookups keyed on the type byte; no memory contract.
Growable heap-backed Squid String; appends are not fixed-buffer writes and it is cleaned before return.

findings.title: Off-by-one out-of-bounds write of NUL terminator past static `line` buffer
findings.invariant: The reconstructed line of length L must be terminated at line[L] (L == number of copied bytes), and the length clamp must reserve index L < TEMP_BUF_SIZE for that NUL. The terminator index must never be L+1.
findings.failure_mode: The clamp at line 80 makes the copied data end at index TEMP_BUF_SIZE-2, so llen can equal TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1 (directly in the else branch, or after `llen += gopherState->len` in the carry branch). Then line[llen + 1] = line[TEMP_BUF_SIZE] writes '\0' one byte past the end of the TEMP_BUF_SIZE static array. A remote Gopher origin server that returns a line of >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1 bytes with no newline forces the clamp and triggers the overrun on demand, corrupting adjacent static/BSS memory — a remotely reachable memory-corruption/DoS primitive.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm TEMP_BUF_SIZE and the LOCAL_ARRAY size, and that no path lets llen exceed TEMP_BUF_SIZE-2 after the clamp; verify the fix writes line[llen] and clamps to TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1 total length.

findings.title: Mis-placed terminator leaves a byte gap, enabling stale-byte over-read by in-place parsers
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read via incorrect NUL termination
findings.invariant: After copying L bytes, line[L] must be '\0' so that strchr/strtok observe a string whose length exactly equals the copied data.
findings.failure_mode: memcpy fills line[0..llen-1] but the terminator is written at line[llen+1], leaving line[llen] holding a leftover byte from a previous, longer line (the success paths at 247/257 do not re-zero `line`). strchr(tline, TAB) and strtok(line, ...) then scan past index llen until the next NUL, so a hostile server can arrange for the stale byte to be a TAB/colon/data byte and cause the field split (gtype/name/selector/host/port or CSO code) to read beyond the intended line, corrupting parsing and reading unintended buffer contents.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm that `line` is not fully re-zeroed on the normal per-line path before parsing, so index llen can carry stale data; verify the corrected code terminates at line[llen].

findings.title: Cross-call reassembly copy assumes gopherState->buf >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write (buffer-size contract on external struct)
findings.invariant: gopherState->buf must be at least TEMP_BUF_SIZE bytes, because the length clamp at line 80 bounds gopherState->len + llen against TEMP_BUF_SIZE, not against sizeof(gopherState->buf).
findings.failure_mode: The partial-line path copies clamped llen bytes into gopherState->buf at offset gopherState->len. The only bound is TEMP_BUF_SIZE. If the GopherStateData definition ever sized buf smaller than TEMP_BUF_SIZE, a server sending a long newline-free chunk would overflow it. This is a latent contract that lives in the struct declaration not shown here.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check the GopherStateData struct: that char buf[] is declared with size >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE and that len can never exceed that size across calls.

findings.title: Unbounded per-line truncation and log spam on hostile Gopher stream
findings.anchor: if (gopherState->len + llen >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE) { debugs(...); llen = TEMP_BUF_SIZE - gopherState->len - 1; }
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled resource consumption / silent data loss
findings.invariant: Over-length lines must be handled without unbounded log volume, and truncation must not silently corrupt security-relevant output; the length math must also leave a correctly-indexed terminator slot.
findings.failure_mode: A malicious origin can send many over-length lines; each logs a DBG_IMPORTANT message and truncates data mid-field, producing log-flooding and silently corrupted HTML. This is the resource/robustness facet distinct from the memory-safety write; it also feeds the flawed terminator arithmetic at line 99.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm whether over-length lines are rate-limited/bounded and that truncation cannot split an escaped field in a way that reintroduces injection.

audit_checklist: Fix line 99 to `line[llen] = '\0'` and ensure the clamp at line 80 reserves index TEMP_BUF_SIZE-1 for it so no write reaches index TEMP_BUF_SIZE.
Confirm the exact value of TEMP_BUF_SIZE and that LOCAL_ARRAY yields a real TEMP_BUF_SIZE-byte array (LOCAL_ARRAY buffers are function-static and reused across calls).
Verify the GopherStateData struct: sizeof(buf) >= TEMP_BUF_SIZE and that gopherState->len can never exceed it across successive gopherToHTML calls.
Ensure `line` is fully re-zeroed (or terminated at exactly the copied length) on every per-line path before strchr/strtok parse it, eliminating stale-byte over-read.
Confirm all snprintf render calls keep the TEMP_BUF_SIZE size argument and that name/host/selector remain html_quote/rfc1738-escaped on every branch (no unescaped interpolation).
Check that atoi(s_code)/atoi(s_recno) in the CSO branch are unreachable with NULL tokens, and that malformed CSO lines cannot desynchronize strtok state.
Rate-limit or cap the DBG_IMPORTANT truncation logging so a hostile server cannot flood logs.
